title: Information for Creditors
description: Essential information for creditors on initiating and conducting enforcement proceedings: documents required, debtor data, types of enforcement, choice of bailiff, and costs.
content:
1. Documents Required to File an Enforcement Application

To initiate enforcement proceedings, you must submit the enforcement title (original required) together with a completed enforcement application at the bailiff's office. When an application is filed by a legal entity, documents confirming the authority of the persons filing the application must also be attached.

To verify that your judgment, order, or payment order constitutes an enforcement title, check whether it contains the following endorsement or stamp:

In the name of the Republic of Poland, on … 20 … the Court … in … certifies that this title authorises enforcement in full/in the scope of … and orders all authorities and persons concerned to comply with the provisions of this title and, when lawfully called upon, to render assistance. The ruling is enforceable as final/immediately enforceable.

Ready-made enforcement application templates are available for download in the 'Forms' section and in paper form at our office reception.

2. Include as Much Debtor Information as Possible in the Application

  • Natural person: NIP (tax ID), PESEL (personal ID), ID card number, residential address,
  • Legal entity: NIP, REGON (statistical number), KRS (court register number), registered office address.

3. Enforcement from Movable Property

In an application for enforcement from movable property, the creditor must specify:

  • the movables subject to enforcement,
  • the address at which the movables are located.

4. Enforcement from Real Estate

  • location of the real estate — address,
  • statement as to whether the debtor is the owner/co-owner,
  • land register (KW) number of the property.

5. Enforcement from Employment Income, Receivables, or Bank Account

If you are requesting enforcement from the debtor's employment income, receivables, or bank account, provide:

  • the name and full address of the debtor's employer,
  • the name and full address of the 'third-party debtor' — the entity against which the debtor has a monetary claim,
  • the name of the bank and/or the debtor's bank account number.

6. Right to Choose a Bailiff

Pursuant to Art. 10 of the Act of 22 March 2018 on Court Bailiffs, the creditor has the right to choose a bailiff within the jurisdiction of the court of appeal, except for cases:

  • concerning enforcement from real estate,
  • concerning the handover of real estate,
  • concerning the introduction into possession of real estate,
  • concerning the clearance of premises, including residential units, of persons or property,
  • in which the provisions on enforcement from real estate apply by analogy.

When making this choice, the creditor submits a written declaration exercising the right to choose a bailiff together with the enforcement application.

7. Commissioning an Asset Search

Every creditor is required to identify the debtor's assets against which enforcement may be conducted. If the creditor does not have this information, they may commission the bailiff to search for the debtor's assets for a fee, pursuant to Art. 8012 of the Code of Civil Procedure.

Pursuant to Art. 44 of the Act of 28 February 2018 on Bailiff Costs, the flat fee for an asset search commissioned by the creditor is PLN 100.

8. Expenses During Enforcement

Pursuant to Art. 5 of the Act of 28 February 2018 on Bailiff Costs, the bailiff is entitled to reimbursement of cash expenses incurred during enforcement only within the scope provided by law. These include:

  1. expert and interpreter fees,
  2. advertising costs,
  3. specialist transport costs,
  4. travel costs outside the bailiff's office location and flat-rate travel costs within that location,
  5. flat-rate costs of recording activities taking place outside the office and storing audio-visual recordings,
  6. fees for persons called under separate regulations to participate in activities,
  7. costs of obtaining documents or information necessary for the proceedings,
  8. correspondence delivery costs (excluding the cost of notifying parties of the initiation of enforcement),
  9. costs of the bailiff's activities outside their district,
  10. costs of transferring funds by postal order or bank transfer,
  11. costs of covering court fees due on an application for entry in the land register.

9. Advance Payment on Expenses

Pursuant to Art. 7(1) of the Act of 28 February 2018 on Bailiff Costs — if a bailiff activity involves expenses, the bailiff conditions performing the activity on the payment of an advance by the party requesting the activity.

10. Service of Procedural Documents

Art. 1391 CCP — Service of the first procedural document by the bailiff

§ 1. If the defendant, despite repeated notification, has not collected the statement of claim or other procedural document requiring a defence of their rights, and no previous document has been served on them in the manner provided in preceding articles, the presiding judge notifies the claimant of this, sending a copy of the document for the defendant and requiring service to be effected through the bailiff.

§ 2. The claimant, within two months from the date of receiving the obligation, files with the court a confirmation of service of the document on the defendant through the bailiff, or returns the document and indicates the defendant's current address or proof that the defendant resides at the address stated in the claim.

Art. 3(4) of the Act on Court Bailiffs

The bailiff, at the court's order or the claimant's request as obligated by the court, personally delivers court notifications, procedural documents, and other court documents directly to the addressee, with acknowledgement of receipt and date, or certifies that the addressee does not reside at the stated address.

Art. 3a — Service through the Bailiff

1. The bailiff performs service within 14 days of receiving the commission.

2. If the addressee is not found at the attempted service, the bailiff establishes whether the addressee resides at the stated address.

3. If the service attempt is unsuccessful but the bailiff has established that the addressee resides at the stated address, a notice of the attempted service with information about the possibility of collecting the document at the bailiff's office within 14 days is placed in the mailbox.

4. If the service attempt is unsuccessful and the addressee does not reside at the stated address, the bailiff returns the document to the commissioning party, informing them of the findings.

Art. 41 of the Act on Bailiff Costs — Service Fee

1. The flat fee for a court order or claimant's request for direct personal service of documents is PLN 60. The fee is charged per delivery to one address of a specified document in the case, regardless of the number of addressees and the number of service attempts.

2. The flat fee for an application to take steps to establish the addressee's current residential address is PLN 40.
metaTitle: Information for Creditors | Court Bailiff Marcin Starzec
